A step-by-step explainer

Density Functional Theoryhow to fake a billion-dimensional problem with a 3-D cloud

By the end you'll understand why chemists and physicists can throw away the impossibly complicated many-electron wavefunction and keep only a single density cloud — and exactly which clever trick makes that legal, accurate, and computable.

For someone fluent in optimization, sufficient statistics, and fixed-point iteration.

The whole idea, in one sentence

Stripped of all the formality, DFT is just the claim that the lowest-energy state of a swarm of interacting electrons is completely pinned down by where the electrons sit on average — a single cloud \(n(\mathbf r)\) in ordinary 3-D space — so you can forget the monstrous many-particle wavefunction and optimize over that cloud instead.

Primer · the wavefunction and its shadow

First, what a wavefunction actually is

Classically a particle has a definite position. Quantum mechanics replaces that with a wavefunction \(\psi(x)\): a complex-valued amplitude attached to every point in space. It is not a probability — it is an amplitude, free to be negative or complex, and that is precisely what lets quantum things interfere.

You never observe \(\psi\) itself. What you observe are particles, and the Born rule is the bridge: the probability of finding the particle near \(x\) is the squared magnitude of the amplitude, \[ p(x) = |\psi(x)|^2, \qquad \int |\psi(x)|^2\,dx = 1. \] So out of the amplitude field falls an ordinary probability density — non-negative and normalized. The wavefunction is the primitive object; the probability distribution is its shadow, obtained by squaring. Squaring is lossy: it discards the phase, which is information the amplitude was carrying. Hold onto that idea — the whole page is about what a distribution forgets and how to get it back.

One last move turns this into the subject at hand. For a single particle \(\psi\) lives over ordinary 3-D space. For \(N\) particles it does not split into \(N\) separate clouds — it is one amplitude \(\Psi(\mathbf r_1,\ldots,\mathbf r_N)\) over the \(3N\)-dimensional configuration space, and \(|\Psi|^2\) is the joint distribution of all \(N\) positions at once. That single fact — one giant joint instead of \(N\) little clouds — is the source of every difficulty the rest of this page exists to dodge.

Top: the complex amplitude \(\psi(x)\) — its real (teal) and imaginary (magenta) parts oscillating inside a Gaussian envelope. Bottom: the Born-rule density \(|\psi(x)|^2\) (amber), with measured particle positions piling into a histogram that converges to it. Slide the momentum \(k\): the amplitude rewinds, yet the probability cloud never moves — the phase is storing information that squaring throws away.

The formal version

A pure state is a unit vector \(|\psi\rangle\) in a Hilbert space (here \(L^2(\mathbb R^3)\)); the position-space wavefunction is the component \(\psi(x)=\langle x|\psi\rangle\). Observables are Hermitian operators, and a position measurement returns \(x\) with density \(|\langle x|\psi\rangle|^2\) — the Born rule — so normalization is just \(\langle\psi|\psi\rangle=1\). The wavepacket above is \(\psi(x)\propto e^{-(x-x_0)^2/4\sigma^2}\,e^{ikx}\): the factor \(e^{ikx}\) sets the mean momentum \(\langle\hat p\rangle=k\) but leaves \(|\psi|^2\) untouched, the cleanest example of phase information invisible to the density.

Step 1 · the wall

The honest answer is uncomputable

Everything about a molecule's ground state is contained in one object: the many-electron wavefunction \(\Psi(\mathbf r_1, \mathbf r_2, \ldots, \mathbf r_N)\), the solution of the time-independent Schrödinger equation \(\hat H \Psi = E\Psi\). The catch is its shape. With \(N\) electrons, \(\Psi\) is a function of \(3N\) coordinates at once.

That is the same monster you know from probabilistic ML: an explicit joint distribution over \(3N\) continuous variables. If you tried to tabulate it on a grid with \(g\) points per axis, you'd need \(g^{3N}\) numbers. This is the exponential wall. Slide \(N\) upward and watch it crash through the number of atoms in the observable universe — for a single caffeine molecule.

Why exponential, when "\(3N\) coordinates" sounds merely linear? Because a function is a rule that must return a value at every point of its domain, and discretizing each axis into \(g\) points turns that domain into a product grid. One electron lives on \(g^3\) cells. For two, the pair \((\mathbf r_1,\mathbf r_2)\) ranges over \(g^3\times g^3\), since for each position of the first the second can independently sit anywhere. Every further electron multiplies the count by another \(g^3\): \[ \underbrace{g^3\times g^3\times\cdots\times g^3}_{N}=g^{3N}. \] The number of arguments grows linearly (\(3N\)); the domain they span grows multiplicatively. That repeated multiplication is the exponential — the curse of dimensionality, nothing more.

You could escape it if \(\Psi\) factorized, \(\Psi=\prod_i \phi_i(\mathbf r_i)\): then \(N\) separate clouds — \(N g^3\) numbers, linear in \(N\) — would rebuild the whole thing. It does not. Coulomb repulsion and Pauli antisymmetry entangle the coordinates, so the amplitude at a joint configuration is genuinely its own number, not deducible from the single-particle pieces. The gap between \(N g^3\) (independent) and \(g^{3N}\) (correlated) is exactly where the interaction lives — the same reason a full joint table over \(D\) variables costs \(g^D\) while an independent model costs only \(D g\).

Each spinning cube is one electron's grid of \(g^3\) possible cells. The joint configuration space is their product: every electron you add multiplies the cell count by another \(g^3\) (amber tape), so \(N\) electrons need \(g^{3N}\) numbers — while \(N\) independent clouds would need only \(N g^3\) (teal). Watch the two diverge as you add electrons.

The amber bar is the full wavefunction \(g^{3N}\); the teal bar is the density \(g^{3}\). The dashed line is \(10^{80}\), roughly every atom in the visible universe.

The formal version

In Hartree atomic units the electronic Hamiltonian is \[ \hat H = -\tfrac12\sum_i \nabla_i^2 + \sum_i v_{\text{ext}}(\mathbf r_i) + \sum_{i

Deep dive · classical point vs quantum cloud

Why the Schrödinger equation scales exponentially

Start classically. Five electrons, each a coloured marble sitting in one cell of a single \(g\times g\times g\) room. The state is just where each marble is: five positions, \(15\) numbers — one point in configuration space. Nothing here grows badly; add a marble, add three numbers.

Quantum mechanics makes each marble delocalize. A quantum electron is no longer at one cell but in a superposition over all \(g^3\) of them at once, and the wavefunction must carry an amplitude for every joint arrangement. Turn the first marble quantum and the number of arrangements is multiplied by \(g^3\) — one copy of the whole configuration for each place that marble could be. Turn the second quantum and you multiply by \(g^3\) again. After all five, \[ \underbrace{g^3\times g^3\times g^3\times g^3\times g^3}_{5}=g^{15}. \] Each electron you promote from point to cloud costs another factor of \(g^3\). That, and nothing more exotic, is the exponential.

Here is the punchline for the equation itself. Solving \(\hat H\Psi=E\Psi\) means finding the unknown \(\Psi\), and \(\Psi\) is a list of one amplitude per configuration — a vector with \(g^{3N}\) entries. So \(\hat H\) is a \(g^{3N}\times g^{3N}\) matrix and "solving Schrödinger" is finding the lowest eigenvector of a matrix exponentially large in the number of electrons. The equation is linear and innocent; it scales exponentially purely because its unknown lives on the configuration space. Classical mechanics tracks one point in that space and stays linear; quantum mechanics tracks a function over all of it and explodes. (The escape, two sections on, is to stop tracking the function and keep only its one-electron marginal.)

A flat \(g\times g\) board, so you can see every cell. Each coloured electron starts classical — one dot in one square. Click to delocalize it and its colour starts twinkling, in its own slot, across every square at once. The tape multiplies by \(g^2\) for each electron you delocalize. (This is the flattened toy: each electron has \(g^2\) cells here versus \(g^3\) in real 3-D space — same multiplication, smaller exponent. The 3-D room below shows the true \(g^3\) version.)

Each coloured marble is one electron. Classical marbles sit still in a single cell. Click to make the next one quantum: it stops being a point and starts twinkling stochastically across every cell of the room — it is now everywhere at once. The tape below multiplies by \(g^3\) for each electron you delocalize, in that electron's colour, building up to \(g^{3N}\) — while the classical description stays at \(15\) numbers.

The formal version

Discretize the single-particle space to \(M=g^3\) sites. A distinguishable-particle basis state is a choice of site for each electron, giving \(M^N=g^{3N}\) basis states; the Hamiltonian is a matrix of that dimension and the ground state is its lowest eigenvector. Antisymmetry for identical fermions trims this to the dimension of the wedge product, \(\binom{M}{N}\) occupied-orbital choices, but for any fixed filling fraction that is still exponential in \(N\) (by Stirling, \(\binom{M}{N}\) grows exponentially). No relabeling rescues you: the eigenproblem's size is exponential in the particle number, which is exactly the content of "the many-body Schrödinger equation is intractable."

Groundwork · a state is a probability distribution

What kind of object are we actually holding?

Before we throw most of \(\Psi\) away, it pays to be precise about what it is — because the whole method is a statement about distributions, marginals and correlation, three things you already know cold.

The wavefunction itself is not the observable thing; the Born rule is what connects it to reality. It says the modulus squared \[ p(\mathbf r_1,\ldots,\mathbf r_N)=|\Psi(\mathbf r_1,\ldots,\mathbf r_N)|^2 \] is a genuine, normalized joint probability density over the \(3N\)-dimensional configuration space — the probability of simultaneously finding electron 1 near \(\mathbf r_1\), electron 2 near \(\mathbf r_2\), and so on, with \(\int p \,d\mathbf r_1\cdots d\mathbf r_N = 1\). So operationally a quantum state is exactly a normalized joint distribution over \(\mathbb R^{3N}\). Every reflex you have about joints, marginals and conditionals transfers verbatim.

With that in hand the "electron density" stops being mysterious — it is simply the one-particle marginal of that joint, scaled by \(N\). Because identical electrons are indistinguishable each one contributes the same marginal, so \[ n(\mathbf r)=N\!\int p(\mathbf r,\mathbf r_2,\ldots,\mathbf r_N)\,d\mathbf r_2\cdots d\mathbf r_N, \qquad \int n(\mathbf r)\,d\mathbf r = N. \] Read it as a slogan: density is to wavefunction as marginal is to joint. The density is nothing but the first moment of the configuration-space distribution — the expected number of electrons per unit volume.

Now the catch, and it is the entire subject in one observation. A marginal is lossy. If the electrons were statistically independent the joint would factorize, \(p=\prod_i p_1(\mathbf r_i)\), and the single density would let you rebuild everything. They are not independent: Coulomb repulsion pushes them apart, and Pauli antisymmetry forbids two identical fermions from sitting in the same place. Both effects make the joint refuse to factorize. The leftover, \(p(\mathbf r_1,\mathbf r_2)-p_1(\mathbf r_1)\,p_1(\mathbf r_2)\), is precisely statistical correlation, and the conditional \(p(\mathbf r_2\mid\mathbf r_1)\) displays it directly: pin one electron down and the others dig a hole around it — the exchange–correlation hole. That hole is the structure the bare density cannot see.

There is a sharper way to see that dependence, one you'll recognize from autoregressive models. Any joint factorizes by the chain rule into a chain of conditionals, \[ p(\mathbf r_1,\ldots,\mathbf r_N)=p(\mathbf r_1)\,p(\mathbf r_2\mid\mathbf r_1)\cdots p(\mathbf r_N\mid\mathbf r_1,\ldots,\mathbf r_{N-1}), \] which is literally a recipe for drawing a configuration: place electron 1 from its marginal, then electron 2 from its law given where 1 landed, and so on. You may sample electron 1 freely, but that choice reshapes the distribution every later electron is drawn from. The density is only the first factor; all the dependence hides in the conditionals.

Equivalently, write the two-electron joint as the independent guess times a correction: \[ p(\mathbf r_1,\mathbf r_2)=\underbrace{p_1(\mathbf r_1)\,p_1(\mathbf r_2)}_{\text{product of marginals}}\; g(\mathbf r_1,\mathbf r_2), \] where the pair-correlation factor \(g\) is a genuine function over the joint plane — the grid the marginals cannot reconstruct. Independence is \(g\equiv 1\); the deviation \(c=p-p_1p_2=p_1p_2\,(g-1)\) is the covariance density, the function-valued generalization of covariance, and \(\log g\) is the pointwise mutual information (its average is \(I(\mathbf r_1;\mathbf r_2)\)). The three buttons on the widget below are exactly these objects: the true joint \(p\), the independent product \(p_1p_2\), and their difference \(c\).

So we appear stuck on a tradeoff: the density is cheap but lossy, and the very correlation we discard is the difficulty of the problem. The astonishing rescue — the content of the next sections — is that for the ground state the density secretly retains enough to recover all of it, and that the lost correlation can be quarantined into a single, comparatively small correction. Keep the widget below in mind throughout: it shows how much lives in the joint that the marginal alone never reveals.

Drag the teal probe to place electron 1 at \(x_1=a\). Top: the selected 2-D field over \((x_1,x_2)\). Bottom, sharing the \(x_2\) axis: the conditional law of electron 2 given that placement (solid amber) against the plain one-electron density (dashed grey). The dip carved into the solid curve right under the probe is the exchange–correlation hole — pure correlation, completely invisible to the density on its own. The difference button shows the covariance density \(c=p-p_1p_2\) directly.

The formal version

The object that captures pair statistics is the pair density, the second-order reduced quantity \[ n_2(\mathbf r,\mathbf r')=N(N-1)\!\int |\Psi|^2\, d\mathbf r_3\cdots d\mathbf r_N , \] the (unnormalized) joint probability of finding one electron at \(\mathbf r\) and another at \(\mathbf r'\). Independence would mean it factorizes, \(n_2(\mathbf r,\mathbf r')=n(\mathbf r)\,n(\mathbf r')\). It does not, and the deviation defines the exchange–correlation hole \(n_{\text{xc}}\): \[ n_2(\mathbf r,\mathbf r')=n(\mathbf r)\big[\,n(\mathbf r')+n_{\text{xc}}(\mathbf r,\mathbf r')\,\big]. \] The hole obeys a sum rule that makes the picture exact: \(\int n_{\text{xc}}(\mathbf r,\mathbf r')\,d\mathbf r' = -1\) — exactly one electron's worth of density is excluded from the neighbourhood of any reference electron. All of the hard physics DFT must eventually approximate is this single deviation from independence.

The same content in the three equivalent forms used above: the pair-correlation factor is \(g=n_2/(n\,n')\) (so \(g\equiv1\) is independence); the connected correlation \(n_2-n\,n'=n\,n_{\text{xc}}\) is the covariance density, whose first moment \(\iint \mathbf r\cdot\mathbf r'\,(n_2-nn')\) is the ordinary covariance; and the sum rule \(\int n_{\text{xc}}\,d\mathbf r'=-1\) is just the statement that conditioning preserves the marginal — the conditional law \(p(\mathbf r'\!\mid\mathbf r)=n(\mathbf r')\,g(\mathbf r,\mathbf r')\) still integrates to one electron once you account for the reference electron removed.

Step 2 · the escape

Collapse the joint into a marginal

Here is the move. Instead of carrying the full joint \(|\Psi|^2\), integrate out all but one electron — exactly a marginalization — and keep only the density: the expected number of electrons at each point of ordinary space, \[ n(\mathbf r) = N\!\int |\Psi(\mathbf r, \mathbf r_2,\ldots,\mathbf r_N)|^2\, d\mathbf r_2\cdots d\mathbf r_N . \] A function of \(3N\) variables becomes a function of \(3\). The dimensionality just evaporated.

Below is the smallest non-trivial case: two electrons living on a line. The joint \(|\Psi(x_1,x_2)|^2\) is already a 2-D heatmap — note the dark valley along the diagonal where \(x_1=x_2\), the exchange hole: identical electrons avoid each other. Press the button to integrate out electron 2 and watch the 2-D joint collapse into the 1-D density curve.

The heatmap is the full two-electron state (2 coordinates). The amber curve along the bottom is everything DFT keeps (1 coordinate).

Step 3 · why it's allowed

The density is a sufficient statistic

Collapsing to the density looks reckless — surely we threw information away? The Hohenberg–Kohn theorem says we did not. The ground-state density \(n(\mathbf r)\) uniquely determines the external potential \(v_{\text{ext}}\) that produced it. And the potential fixes the Hamiltonian, which fixes \(\Psi\), which fixes every observable. So the chain \[ n(\mathbf r)\;\longleftrightarrow\; v_{\text{ext}}\;\longrightarrow\; \hat H\;\longrightarrow\;\Psi\;\longrightarrow\;\text{everything} \] is one-to-one. In your language: the density is a lossless sufficient statistic for the entire ground state. Nothing was lost — it was just re-coded into 3-D.

Reshape the potential below (the teal curve) and watch the ground-state density (the amber cloud) respond. The map is invertible: no two distinct potentials ever yield the same density, so reading the density backwards recovers the potential.

Teal = the potential you sculpt, \(v_{\text{ext}}(x)\). Amber = the ground-state density it forces, \(n(x)=|\psi_0(x)|^2\), solved live by imaginary-time relaxation.

The formal version

Suppose two potentials \(v_1, v_2\) (differing by more than a constant) gave the same ground-state density \(n\), with ground states \(\Psi_1,\Psi_2\) and energies \(E_1,E_2\). The variational principle gives \(E_1 < \langle\Psi_2|\hat H_1|\Psi_2\rangle = E_2 + \int (v_1-v_2)\,n\), and symmetrically \(E_2 < E_1 + \int (v_2-v_1)\,n\). Adding yields \(E_1+E_2 < E_1+E_2\), a contradiction. Hence \(n \mapsto v_{\text{ext}}\) is injective (Hohenberg–Kohn I, 1964).

Step 4 · the optimization

Turn physics into a variational problem

Because every observable is a functional of the density, so is the energy: \(E[n]\). And the second Hohenberg–Kohn theorem hands you a loss to minimize — the true ground-state density is the one that makes \(E[n]\) smallest, and that minimum is the ground-state energy: \[ E_0 = \min_{n}\; E[n], \qquad E[n] = \underbrace{T[n] + V_{ee}[n]}_{\text{universal }F[n]} + \int v_{\text{ext}}(\mathbf r)\,n(\mathbf r)\,d\mathbf r . \] A quantum eigenvalue problem became a constrained optimization over a 3-D function.

Feel it for a single electron in a well. Drag the width \(s\) of a trial density cloud. A skinny cloud is cheap in potential energy but expensive in kinetic energy (sharp curvature); a fat cloud is the reverse. The energy is a tug-of-war, and it bottoms out at exactly the true ground state.

kinetic T[n]—
potential ∫v n—
total E[n]—

Left: the trial density (amber) sitting in the well (teal). Right: the energy \(E[s]\) traced live; the hollow marker is the true minimum. Drag \(s\) to find it.

The formal version

For a single occupied orbital the kinetic functional is exact and local — the von Weizsäcker form \(T[n]=\tfrac12\int |\nabla\sqrt{n}|^2\,d\mathbf r\). The demo uses \(v(x)=\tfrac12 x^2\), whose exact ground-state density is \(n\propto e^{-x^2}\) (width \(s=1/\sqrt2\approx0.707\)), giving \(E_0=\tfrac12\). The catch for many interacting electrons: nobody knows an accurate explicit \(T[n]\). That gap is what Step 5 repairs.

Step 5 · the Kohn–Sham trick

Quarantine the hard part

Step 4 hid a landmine: for interacting electrons we have no accurate formula for the kinetic energy \(T[n]\) in terms of the density alone. Kohn and Sham's escape is a reparameterization you'll recognize. Invent a fictitious system of non-interacting electrons, tuned to have the exact same density as the real one. Non-interacting electrons live in single-particle orbitals \(\psi_i\), so their kinetic energy \(T_s\) is computable exactly.

Now split the energy into pieces you can compute — the non-interacting kinetic energy, the external potential, and the classical electrostatic (Hartree) self-repulsion of the cloud — and sweep everything you can't into a single correction term, the exchange–correlation functional \(E_{xc}[n]\). Press through the budget below: the hard physics ends up quarantined in one small sliver, which is why even a crude guess for \(E_{xc}\) gives excellent total energies.

It is worth naming what \(E_{xc}\) is in probabilistic terms, because it closes the loop with the correlation from the Groundwork section. The Hartree term is the independent energy: it feeds the product of marginals \(n(\mathbf r)n(\mathbf r')\) into the Coulomb repulsion, as if the density were a charge cloud interacting with itself. \(E_{xc}\) is the bill for everything that approximation got wrong — and it is the same covariance density \(c=p-p_1p_2\), only now contracted against the Coulomb kernel \(1/r\) instead of against \(\mathbf r_1\mathbf r_2\). Where ordinary covariance asks how the positions co-vary, \(E_{xc}\) asks how much Coulomb energy that co-variation saves: each electron carves a hole holding exactly one electron's worth of missing density, and \(E_{xc}\) is the favourable interaction of the electron with its own hole.

Schematic energy budget for a small molecule. Three terms are known exactly; the magenta sliver \(E_{xc}\) is the only thing we must approximate.

The formal version

The Kohn–Sham decomposition is \[ E[n] = T_s[n] + \int v_{\text{ext}}\,n + \tfrac12\!\iint\frac{n(\mathbf r)n(\mathbf r')}{|\mathbf r-\mathbf r'|}\,d\mathbf r\,d\mathbf r' + E_{xc}[n], \] which defines \(E_{xc}\) as everything left over: the kinetic correction \(T[n]-T_s[n]\) plus the non-classical part of the interaction. The whole approach is exact — the only approximation in practice is the choice of \(E_{xc}\). Standard rungs of "Jacob's ladder": LDA \(\big(E_{xc}=\int n\,\epsilon_{xc}(n)\big)\), GGA (adds \(\nabla n\), e.g. PBE), meta-GGA, and hybrids that mix in exact exchange (e.g. B3LYP).

The probabilistic reading is exact. The potential part of \(E_{xc}\) is the connected correlation contracted with the Coulomb kernel, \[ V_{ee}-E_H=\tfrac12\!\iint\frac{n_2(\mathbf r,\mathbf r')-n(\mathbf r)n(\mathbf r')}{|\mathbf r-\mathbf r'|} =\tfrac12\!\iint\frac{n(\mathbf r)\,n_{\text{xc}}(\mathbf r,\mathbf r')}{|\mathbf r-\mathbf r'|}, \] the very same numerator \(n_2-n\,n'\) whose \(\mathbf r_1\mathbf r_2\) moment is the covariance — identical correlation, different kernel. Exchange is the part of the hole forced by antisymmetry (the Fermi hole, present even at zero interaction in the Kohn–Sham determinant); correlation is the remainder switched on by real repulsion (the Coulomb hole). The kinetic correction \(T-T_s\) folds in via the adiabatic connection, which replaces \(n_{\text{xc}}\) by a coupling-constant-averaged hole \(\bar n_{\text{xc}}\); after that substitution the identity above for the full \(E_{xc}\) is exact.

Step 6 · the algorithm

Solve it by chasing a fixed point

Minimizing the Kohn–Sham energy gives single-particle equations — one effective Schrödinger equation per orbital, \[ \Big[-\tfrac12\nabla^2 + v_{\text{eff}}(\mathbf r)\Big]\psi_i = \varepsilon_i\,\psi_i, \qquad v_{\text{eff}} = v_{\text{ext}} + v_{H}[n] + v_{xc}[n]. \] The many-body interaction has become a mean field: each electron simply moves in the averaged potential created by the whole density. This is exactly mean-field variational inference wearing a lab coat.

But \(v_{\text{eff}}\) is built from \(n\), and \(n\) is built from the \(\psi_i\), which come from \(v_{\text{eff}}\). It's a fixed-point problem, solved by iteration: guess a density, build the potential, solve for orbitals, get a new density, mix, repeat until nothing changes — the self-consistent field (SCF) loop. Step through a genuine 4-electron calculation below and watch the density and potential settle, with the convergence error collapsing.

iteration0
‖Δn‖ (self-consistency error)—

Top: effective potential \(v_{\text{eff}}\) (teal) with occupied orbital energies as dashed levels. Bottom: the electron density \(n(x)\) (amber). A real SCF loop: external trap + Hartree repulsion + LDA exchange.

Putting it back together

The kernel was: the ground state is fixed by a 3-D density cloud, so optimize over the cloud. Every step was that sentence acquiring rigor. We hit the exponential wall (Step 1), escaped by marginalizing the joint down to the density (Step 2), and learned from Hohenberg–Kohn that this density is a lossless sufficient statistic (Step 3). That let us recast the physics as a variational minimization of a density functional (Step 4). The one hard term — the interacting kinetic energy — we dodged by Kohn and Sham's reparameterization: a fake non-interacting system with the same density, with all the genuinely hard physics quarantined into a small \(E_{xc}\) (Step 5). And we solved the resulting mean-field equations by fixed-point iteration, the SCF loop (Step 6).

In one breath: sufficient statistic, reparameterize the intractable term, dump the residual into one small correction you approximate, then iterate to a fixed point. A physicist would call it DFT; you've been doing the same dance for years.

Where this shows up

Materials & drug discovery

DFT is the workhorse of computational chemistry and condensed-matter physics — predicting reaction energies, band structures, catalysts, and battery materials at a cost that scales like \(N^3\) instead of exponentially. It earned Walter Kohn the 1998 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

The exchange–correlation functional is a learning problem

The one unknown, \(E_{xc}[n]\), is exactly an inductive bias to be designed — or learned. Hand-crafted functionals (LDA, PBE, B3LYP) are giving way to neural functionals trained on reference data (e.g. DeepMind's DM21), turning the last gap in DFT into a supervised regression problem.

The same pattern, everywhere

Replace an intractable joint with a sufficient statistic, reparameterize the hard term through a tractable auxiliary model, and iterate a mean field to self-consistency: that recipe recurs in variational inference, EM, amortized inference, and mean-field game theory. DFT is one especially successful instance.
